subject: science
title: science_3.json
description: Grade 4 Science Assessment - Day 3: Energy, Forces, and Magnets

questions.q: What type of energy is stored in an object due to its position or height above the ground?
options: Potential energy
Kinetic energy
Thermal energy
Sound energy
questions.hint: Think about a roller coaster car sitting completely still at the very top of a steep hill.
questions.explanation: Potential energy is stored energy based on an object's position or structure, which can be released as kinetic energy when it moves.

questions.q: When a ball rolls down a smooth hill, what energy transformation is primarily taking place?
options: Potential energy is converting into kinetic energy.
Kinetic energy is converting into chemical energy.
Electrical energy is converting into potential energy.
Thermal energy is converting into nuclear energy.
questions.answer: Potential energy is converting into kinetic energy.
questions.hint: The stored energy of being high up changes into the energy of active motion.
questions.explanation: As the ball moves downward, its height decreases (losing potential energy) and its speed increases (gaining kinetic energy).

questions.q: What force acts between two surfaces rubbing against each other, working to slow down moving objects?
questions.hint: Rub your hands together quickly; this force causes them to warm up.
questions.explanation: Friction is a resistive force that opposes motion when two physical surfaces slide or roll past one another.

questions.q: Why do ice skates slide smoothly over ice while sneakers grip the gym floor?
options: The blades create less friction with ice than rubber soles do with wood.
Ice eliminates gravity entirely for the skater.
Sneakers produce more potential energy than thin metal blades.
The gym floor creates a strong magnetic pull on the sneakers.
questions.answer: The blades create less friction with ice than rubber soles do with wood.
questions.hint: Think about which combination of surfaces allows things to slide easily.
questions.explanation: Smooth metal blades on slick ice generate minimal friction, allowing easy gliding, whereas textured rubber soles create high friction to prevent slipping.

questions.q: What will happen if you bring the north pole of one bar magnet close to the north pole of another bar magnet?
options: They will push away from each other.
They will stick firmly together.
They will cancel each other out and lose magnetism.
They will rotate until they point directly toward the moon.
questions.answer: They will push away from each other.
questions.hint: Remember the fundamental law of magnetism: 'Like poles...'
questions.explanation: Like magnetic poles (North-North or South-South) repel each other, while opposite poles attract.

questions.q: What mineral or natural magnetic rock was first discovered by ancient civilizations to attract iron pieces?
questions.hint: It translates to 'leading stone' and contains the mineral magnetite.
questions.explanation: Lodestone is a naturally magnetized piece of the mineral magnetite, which historical navigators used as early compasses.

questions.q: If you wrap a copper wire around an iron nail and attach the ends to a battery, you have created a temporary magnet called a(n):
options: Electromagnet
Permanent magnet
Natural magnet
Bar magnet
questions.hint: Its power relies entirely on the flow of electricity through the wire loop.
questions.explanation: An electromagnet is a type of magnet where the magnetic field is produced by an electric current running through a coil.

questions.q: Which of the following objects would be strongly attracted to a household refrigerator magnet?
options: A steel paperclip
A plastic ruler
A copper penny
A wooden toothpick
questions.hint: Magnets attract specific metals like iron, nickel, and steel.
questions.explanation: Steel contains iron, which is a ferromagnetic material that responds strongly to magnetic fields.

questions.q: How does an engineer increase the strength of a basic school-project electromagnet?
options: By winding more coils of wire around the central core
By shortening the wire so it stays away from the nail
By switching to a smaller plastic core instead of iron
By cooling the battery down in an ice bath
questions.answer: By winding more coils of wire around the central core
questions.hint: Think about how adding more loops multiplies the magnetic effect of the electrical current.
questions.explanation: Increasing the number of wire turns around the core concentrates the magnetic field lines, making the electromagnet significantly stronger.

questions.q: What invisible area surrounding a magnet exerts a force on other magnetic materials?
questions.hint: It is the region where magnetic lines of force can be detected.
questions.explanation: The magnetic field is the spatial region around a magnetic material where the magnetic force is active and measurable.
